blocks.content: Ethan ran the same route the next morning.
Or at least, that was what he told himself.

Same streets. Same cool air. Same stretch of older buildings leaning into the day before the sun had fully made up its mind. His shoes tapped against the pavement in the same steady rhythm, and the town unfolded around him like it always did—quiet, patient, almost untouched.
But something was different.
Not the street.
Not the morning.
Him.
Ethan slowed before he meant to.

The park sat at the end of the block, still tucked behind its old stone archway, still modest enough that most people passed it without giving it more than a glance. The fountain in the center sputtered weakly, sending up a thin stream of water that broke apart before it had the dignity to become anything impressive.
Yesterday, it had just been a fountain.
Old. Uneven. A little sad.
Today, it felt like a page someone had turned and left open.
Ethan stood near the edge of the sidewalk, breathing harder than the run required.
That fountain looks better in your painting than it does in real life.
Her voice came back first.
Not her face.
Not her red hair.
Not the way she smiled like she had already decided the world was worth interrupting.
Her voice.
Light, teasing, unexpected.
The kind of voice that walked straight into a quiet moment and changed the shape of it.
Ethan looked toward the bench near the archway.
Empty.
Of course it was empty.
What had he expected?
That she would be there again, holding that ridiculous purple dinosaur like some victorious general returning from war?
He almost smiled.
Then immediately felt stupid for smiling.
It was three minutes,
he reminded himself.
Maybe less.
A stranger had commented on his painting. They had exchanged names. She had made him laugh. That was all.
Except it wasn't all.
He had painted differently after she left.
That was the problem.
Ethan could explain away a face. A laugh. A pretty girl on a bright morning.
But he could not explain the way the light on his canvas had changed.
He stared at the fountain a little longer.
The water coughed upward again and collapsed back into itself.
Artists are allowed to be hopeful.
He exhaled through his nose.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: That was a weird thing to say to a stranger,
he muttered.
But the words did not feel weird.
They felt like something he had been handed without knowing what to do with it.
A jogger passed behind him, and Ethan realized he was standing still in the middle of his run, staring at a fountain like it had personally betrayed him.
He started moving again.
But slower this time.
And when he left the park behind, it did not feel like he had passed it.
It felt like he was carrying it with him.
